overview: FHRP (First Hop Redundancy Protocol) provides a virtual IP and virtual MAC address shared across redundant routers, so hosts keep a single, stable default gateway even if the active router fails.
definitions: Active router (HSRP): the router currently responding to ARP requests for the virtual IP with the virtual MAC.
Standby router (HSRP): listens for hellos from the Active router and takes over if it fails.
core_concepts: HSRP and VRRP both use an Active/Standby model with per-subnet load balancing (only one router forwards per subnet at a time).
GLBP uses an Active/Active model, load-balancing per host in round-robin fashion across multiple routers.
protocol_operation: Active router answers ARP requests for the virtual IP with the virtual MAC.
Standby router listens for periodic hellos; if hellos stop, it becomes Active.
packet_flow: Host ARPs for the default gateway's virtual IP -> Active router replies with the virtual MAC -> host sends traffic to the virtual MAC -> Active router forwards it -> if Active fails, Standby detects missed hellos and takes over the virtual IP/MAC seamlessly.
best_practices: Use GLBP instead of HSRP/VRRP when you specifically need active/active load balancing across redundant gateways.
interview_questions: Compare the load-balancing model of HSRP/VRRP versus GLBP.
What does the Standby router do while the Active router is healthy?
exam_traps: Assuming HSRP naturally load-balances traffic across both routers — it does not; only one router forwards per subnet in HSRP/VRRP.
enterprise_notes: FHRP is standard on any redundant distribution-layer pair providing default gateway service to access-layer VLANs.
